scenario: A SaaS-based pricing engine determines monthly subscription tiers based on the customer's account age and usage. The DMN Decision Table pricing-tier takes inputs accountAgeMonths (integer) and usageTier (string: "low", "medium", "high"). The output is subscriptionTier (string: "starter", "growth", "enterprise"). The team needs the table to handle the case where accountAgeMonths is less than 0 (data quality issue) by defaulting to "starter". The developer is debating between two approaches: 1. Add a special rule at the top with accountAgeMonths < 0 → "starter". 2. Use the Hit Policy default semantics.
question: Which approach is BPMN/DMN-correct for default handling in a Decision Table?

options.text: Add a catch-all rule at the bottom of the table with - (don't-care) for both inputs and "starter" as output. With FIRST hit policy, this fires only if no specific rule matched above. The - (dash or blank) in a DMN input cell is the "match anything" wildcard -- the cell imposes no constraint on that column. A rule with - in every input column therefore matches any input combination that fell through the preceding rules. Combined with FIRST hit policy (which returns the first matching rule by row order), the catch-all serves as a universal fallback -- covering both the accountAgeMonths < 0 case and any other unhandled input combination.

Add an explicit "first-line" rule with accountAgeMonths < 0 → "starter". Combined with FIRST hit policy, this rule fires when account age is invalid; otherwise the regular rules apply. , usageTier = "unknown") without a fallback. The catch-all approach with - is more comprehensive and idiomatic.

Configure the Hit Policy to DEFAULT which is automatically applied when no other rule matches. 3 specification or in Camunda 8. md:26-53, Camunda 8 supports exactly: UNIQUE, ANY, FIRST, RULE ORDER, and COLLECT. No automatic "use a default output when nothing matches" mechanism exists as a hit policy setting; the canonical workaround is the catch-all row with - values.

Use FEEL default keyword in the output cell of the last rule. There is no default keyword in FEEL syntax for DMN output cells. FEEL output cells in a decision table contain FEEL value expressions -- strings, numbers, booleans, context literals, or FEEL built-in function calls. The concept of "fire when nothing else matches" is achieved structurally by the catch-all row (option a) combined with FIRST hit policy, not by any special keyword placed inside a cell's value expression.

a.text: Correct. An explicit invalid-data rule at the top works for accountAgeMonths < 0 cases but is too narrow -- it doesn't handle other "no rule matched" cases (e.g., usageTier = "unknown").

b.text: Incorrect. DMN does not have a "DEFAULT" hit policy. The Camunda 8 supported Hit Policies are UNIQUE, ANY, FIRST, RULE ORDER, COLLECT (per decision-table-hit-policy.md:26-53). There is no "default" mode.

c.text: Incorrect. A catch-all rule at the bottom with - (don't-care, matches anything) in each input column is the DMN-canonical way to provide a default. Combined with FIRST hit policy (which returns the first matching rule by row order), the catch-all only fires if no preceding specific rule matched. This handles both the explicit invalid-data case (no rule matches accountAgeMonths < 0) and any other unmatched input combinations.

d.text: Incorrect. There is no default keyword in FEEL output cell syntax. Output cells contain expressions whose value is the rule's output when fired.

explanation: An explicit invalid-data rule at the top works for accountAgeMonths < 0 cases but is too narrow -- it doesn't handle other "no rule matched" cases (e.g., usageTier = "unknown").

findings.message: option text contains its own verdict/rationale
